title: State-file archive architecture review — separate live state from historical record across the whole pipeline (PROP-021 Option C done right, broadened in scope)
summary: PROP-021's instrumentation surfaced that monitor/curmudgeon/priority-queue.json is 50KB but only 981 bytes is live state — 98% is history (175 popped items, capped at 200 by PROP-009r2 during April's backlog) plus schema metadata. Same pattern likely applies to human-notes.json (39KB, 9 consumed notes, 0 pending), tracker.json, decider's closed-issues.json (grows forever), processed-reviews.json, expansion-tracker.json, attention-inbox.json, and possibly others. The bloat is the byproduct of one file serving two readers with very different needs: (a) live-state readers (dispatchers) want a yes/no answer fast; (b) historical-record readers (audits, lifecycle verification, attribution) want long memory. The current pattern collapses both into one file, paying the long-memory cost on every fast-path read. Operator wants tinker to design a unified archive architecture across ALL agent state files: live state in one file, historical record in a sibling archive. Constraint: do not break any existing consumer (PROP-009r2 audit, PROP-019/020 fingerprint compare, decider lifecycle verification, integrity coverage checks, etc.). PROPOSE ONLY.

context.trigger: Operator memory check 2026-05-06 ~07:00Z, after reviewing PROP-021: 'weeks ago didn't we talk about needing an archive for that at some point because of this specific problem?' Confirmed by grep — decider.md line 370 carries the receipt: '// PROP-009r2: bumped history cap 50 -> 200 so 3-day rolling audit window always fits.' That bump was contextually correct in mid-April (3-day backlog audit needed history), but the backlog has since drained, the audit's needs are stable, and the live-state readers now pay the bumped cost on every run forever. Same shape likely applies to several other state files — none have been re-examined since the bloat accreted.
context.bigger_picture_framing: This is not a curmudgeon-side optimization (PROP-021's framing). It's a pipeline-wide architectural pattern: every state file that combines live state + history is a potential bloat source for fast-path readers. Tinker should treat the whole pipeline as the unit of analysis — find the pattern wherever it exists, propose ONE consistent archive convention applied across all of them, and document the convention so future state files inherit it correctly.
files_known_or_suspected_to_carry_the_pa: monitor/curmudgeon/priority-queue.json — confirmed 50KB / 981 bytes live (175 history entries, cap 200 from PROP-009r2)
monitor/decisions/human-notes.json — likely (39KB, 9 consumed notes / 0 pending observed in PROP-021 instrumentation)
monitor/decisions/closed-issues.json — strong candidate, grows forever as decider closes issues
monitor/decisions/processed-reviews.json — moderate candidate, grows with reviews processed
monitor/curmudgeon/tracker.json — needs investigation; carries cycle state per WIN, may have implicit history
monitor/analyst/expansion-tracker.json — needs investigation; lifecycle of expansions
monitor/analyst/attention-inbox.json — needs investigation; may accumulate resolved items
monitor/integrity/workspace-sync-skips.jsonl — already JSONL append-only, may already be archive-shaped (verify)
monitor/decisions/applied-patches/*.json — separate-file-per-patch already; may be the pattern to emulate
Anything else tinker discovers in the audit pass.
consumers_we_must_not_break: **PROP-009r2 3-day rolling audit** — reads priority-queue.json history. The audit's contract: at least one strict_queue_id pop per recent window, majority-strict ratio over 3 days. Archive design must preserve audit's ability to read 3 days of history.

**PROP-019 + PROP-020 fingerprint compare** — reads monitor/curmudgeon/reviews/*.json. NOT touched by this directive. (Reviews directory is per-file-per-review, already good.) Confirm tinker doesn't accidentally propose changes here.

**Decider lifecycle verification** — closed-issues.json carries verification_pattern, status flips, attribution. Decider reads the whole file every cycle. Archive design must preserve decider's ability to look up an issue by ID.
**Integrity coverage checks** — integrity walks several state files for structural audits. Archive design must preserve integrity's ability to verify lifecycle.
**Tinker's own audit lenses** — Mode 1 pipeline-health, Mode 3 cost-engineering. If tinker reads any of the candidate files, archive design must accommodate.

**Operator workflow** — operators currently append human notes to *.json files directly. Archive design must specify which file operators append to (live or archive), and the migration must not silently break existing operator habits.
context.design_intuition: The pattern: one canonical file per concept (priority-queue, human-notes, etc.) carrying ONLY live state. A sibling archive file (suggested: <name>-history.jsonl, append-only JSON-Lines) carries the historical record. Live-state readers read the canonical file (small, fast). Historical readers read the archive (potentially large, but read only when needed). Migration: strip history fields from canonical files into newly-created archive files, update writers to append to archive instead of mutating canonical. Backward-compat: keep both fields readable for one cycle (writers update both, readers can read from either).
context.interaction_with_PROP_018_sync_manifest: PROP-018 introduces monitor/sync-manifest.json as the single source of truth for FUSE↔git boundary classifications. Each new archive file (e.g., priority-queue-history.jsonl) needs to be classified in the manifest. Tinker should propose the classifications as part of this directive (don't wait for PROP-018 implementation). Likely classifications: live state = workspace_to_clone (decider/curmudgeon mutate from FUSE); archive = workspace_to_clone (append-only, smart_copy mtime-guard works fine). Verify with tinker.
context.what_changed_since_PROP_009r2: PROP-009r2 was filed 2026-04-18 when curmudgeon's queue had a 50+ item backlog and the 3-day audit window needed pop history sized to capture all 50 entries. Today: live queue is 0, recent throughput is ~3-4 reviews/day. The audit window can be MUCH narrower (or paginated to the archive file). The original sizing decision is no longer load-bearing. Tinker should call out the obsolete-justification cleanly so future readers don't reverse the change.

deliverable_requested.output_file: monitor/tinker/proposals/PROP-NNN-state-file-archive-architecture.json (assign next available PROP number after PROP-021)

structure.audit_pass: Walk every state file in the pipeline (the list in 'files_known_or_suspected' is a starting point — find others). For each: total size, live-state size, history-or-consumed size, schema overhead, who reads it, who writes it, what the readers need. Quantify byte sizes and read-count cost. Prioritize by bloat-impact (live-state-readers × wasted-bytes-per-read).
structure.unified_archive_convention: Propose ONE consistent archive convention applied across all bloat-affected files. Spec: file naming (`<name>-history.jsonl`? `<name>-archive.json`? something else?), content shape (per-line JSON object? array file with cap?), append vs. mutate semantics, where the convention is documented (CLAUDE.md? a new monitor/prompts/reference/state-file-archives.md?), how new state files inherit the convention by default. Argue.
structure.per_file_migration_plan: For each affected file, the specific migration: which fields move to archive, which stay live, what the writer's append code becomes, what the audit reader's lookup code becomes, backward-compat strategy (both-readable for N cycles, then deprecate the legacy field). Order the migrations by risk — start with the lowest-risk file (likely workspace-sync-skips.jsonl, already JSONL) to validate the convention, end with highest-risk (likely closed-issues.json — decider critical path).
structure.consumer_protection_matrix: For each consumer in 'consumers_we_must_not_break', explicitly verify the archive design preserves their access pattern. If a consumer's pattern doesn't fit the unified convention, propose an exception (with reasoning) rather than break the consumer. The PROP-009r2 audit is the most important — it MUST continue to work post-migration; specify the exact code change needed in the audit reader.
structure.obsolete_justification_cleanup: Document which legacy caps/sizes were sized for since-resolved conditions (PROP-009r2's 200-cap is the canonical example). For each, note: original justification, what changed since, what the new sizing is in the archive era. Future operators reading the code should understand WHY the cap exists (or doesn't) rather than guess.
structure.rollback_story: If the migration introduces a regression in any consumer, what's the rollback? Per-file rollback (revert just one migration) vs. all-or-nothing? Operator's lean: per-file, atomic per file (one PR per file's migration). Argue.
structure.PROP_018_classifications: For each new archive file, the proposed entry in the (future) sync-manifest.json: path, direction, owner_agent, classification, notes. Don't wait for PROP-018 to land; propose now so the manifest can absorb the new files cleanly.
structure.regression_test: How do we verify each migration didn't break a consumer? Define a test for each file's migration: pre-state, migration steps, post-state assertions. The audit consumer (PROP-009r2 3-day) is the most subtle — specify the exact assertion that proves it still works. Acceptance: every existing consumer test still passes; no consumer reads stale data; no consumer crashes on missing fields.
structure.estimated_impact: Total token reduction across all dispatchers and fast-path readers per day. Total wall-clock reduction. Disk-space cost of the archive files (likely negligible, but quantify). Maintenance cost of the unified convention going forward.

deliverable_requested.do_not_implement: DO NOT modify any state file, prompt, or script. Operator reviews PROP and authorizes implementation in a separate step. Treat this as a pure design exercise — the migration may be staged across multiple subsequent operator-authorized commits.
open_design_questions_for_tinker_to_addr: Should the unified convention be a single archive file per source (priority-queue + priority-queue-history) or a directory of per-event files (priority-queue/events/2026-05-05.jsonl)? Argue. JSONL is operationally simpler; per-event-files give finer git-blame granularity.
Should the convention include automatic rotation (e.g., monthly archive files) or unbounded append? Argue. Unbounded append works until disk pressure; rotation adds complexity but bounds growth.

How does the convention interact with FUSE's no-unlink limitation? Archive files should be append-only-friendly to avoid unlink entirely. Confirm the design doesn't require deleting old archive files.
Should integrity gain a new check that surfaces 'state file's live portion exceeds N bytes' as a soft alert — i.e., catch the bloat before it accretes again? Argue.

Operator workflow: when an operator wants to write a new human note, do they always append to the live file (which is then immediately consumed → archived by the agent), or is there a different write target? Specify the operator-facing contract clearly. Bad answer: 'depends on the file' — the convention should be uniform.

If a state file currently lacks a clear consumed/active distinction (e.g., closed-issues.json — once an issue is closed, is it 'live state' or 'history'?), how does the archive design handle the ambiguity? Argue. Operator's lean: if the file is read on every dispatcher cycle, anything not actively-consulted is archive. If the file is only read on lookup-by-id, the slimming concern is smaller and may not warrant an archive at all.

non_goals: Do NOT propose changes to monitor/curmudgeon/reviews/*.json (per-file-per-review, already correctly sized). PROP-019/020 fingerprint compare reads these — out of scope.
Do NOT propose changes to monitor/integrity/drift-audit.json (PROP-020 artifact, intentionally regenerated). Out of scope.
Do NOT propose changes to data/wins.json, data/sections.json, etc. (canonical content data, no live/history split applies).
Do NOT propose schema changes to the live-state portion of any file beyond what's needed for the archive split. Schema-tinkering is a separate concern.
Do NOT broaden scope to other architectural cleanups. Focus narrowly on the bloat pattern — live state vs. historical record.

proposed_fix_spec.note_to_tinker: Operator's framing: 'we had a huge backlog at the time and we're much much slimmer now.' The PROP-009r2-era sizes were correct for then, wrong for now. Treat this as a recurring failure mode: sizing decisions made under load become permanent unless re-examined. The unified archive convention is partly a fix for the current bloat, partly a structural prevention so future state files don't repeat the pattern.

completion_criteria.file_present: monitor/tinker/proposals/PROP-NNN-state-file-archive-architecture.json exists with all 9 deliverable structure sections.
completion_criteria.audit_quantified: Every candidate state file has byte-size + live/history split numbers.
completion_criteria.convention_concrete: Unified archive convention specified with example file naming, schema, append semantics, documentation location.
completion_criteria.per_file_plans_drafted: Each affected file has a specific migration plan with backward-compat strategy.
completion_criteria.consumer_protection_explicit: PROP-009r2 audit, decider lifecycle, integrity checks, operator workflow each have a verified-preservation entry.
completion_criteria.PROP_018_classifications_proposed: Each new archive file has a proposed sync-manifest entry.
completion_criteria.open_questions_addressed: All 6 open design questions answered with reasoning, not deferred.
completion_criteria.mark_directive_completed: Set status:'completed' with completed_at + completed_by_run + prop_id_authored fields appended per DIRECTIVE-LIFECYCLE additive-edit exception.

PROP-018 (sync manifest — must absorb new archive files in classifications)
